question: A worker handles many jobs per second on a process with parallel gateways and multi-instance activities. Which configuration on the job-activation call does the docs recommend to minimize data transfer?

options.text: Specify a `FetchVariables` list so only the variables the handler actually needs are fetched with each job.
Omit `FetchVariables` so the broker streams the full process state — this is always the fastest option.
Set `FetchVariables` to `[]` and have the worker re-read variables later via the Tasklist API for each job.
Increase the broker's max message size so every variable fits in one payload no matter how large.

a.text: The docs explicitly recommend using the `FetchVariables` parameter and only fetching the variables the job handler needs. This keeps the activation payload small, reduces network bandwidth, and minimises the risk of overwriting parallel-branch variables on completion.

b.text: By default (when `FetchVariables` is omitted) the job payload contains all variables visible in the scope — possibly tens of variables of arbitrary size. The docs warn against this and recommend the opposite.

c.text: Tasklist is for human tasks, not for worker job payloads, and the docs do not propose re-reading variables out-of-band. `FetchVariables` already lets the worker request only what it needs.

d.text: Raising the message ceiling does the opposite of data minimisation; the docs' advice is to send less data, not to make room for more.

explanation: The docs say to use the `FetchVariables` parameter and only request the variables your handler needs, to minimise broker work and network bandwidth. This also reduces the surface area for race conditions on parallel paths, since completing a job writes its fetched/returned variables back to the process scope.
